title: React Fiber
tldr: React Fiber is a complete rewrite of React's reconciliation engine, first released in React 16.
It enables incremental rendering - splitting rendering work into chunks spread over multiple frames.
Fiber introduces a tree of 'fiber nodes' representing units of work, with linked lists for traversal.
Features enabled: concurrent mode, Suspense, error boundaries, and prioritized updates.
laymanDefinition: Imagine assembling a huge Lego castle but you must finish in one go - that was the old Stack reconciler. Now imagine you can pause, answer a phone call (higher priority), then resume exactly where you left off - that's Fiber.

deepDive.heading: The Problem with Stack Reconciler
deepDive.text: The old Stack reconciler used recursive synchronous traversal. Once started, it blocked the main thread until completion, causing jank in animations and poor UX.

deepDive.heading: Fiber Node Architecture
deepDive.text: A Fiber is a JS object representing a unit of work. Key properties: tag, type, key, child, sibling, return (parent), pendingProps, memoizedProps, memoizedState, effectTag, alternate. Fibers form a linked list tree.

deepDive.heading: Work Loop and Scheduling
deepDive.text: Fiber uses cooperative scheduling. The work loop processes one fiber unit at a time, checking shouldYield() after each unit. If higher-priority work exists, it yields to the browser.

deepDive.heading: Priority Levels
deepDive.text: Immediate (mouse events), UserBlocking (keyboard, scroll), Normal (network, state), Low (prefetching), Idle (analytics). Higher-priority interrupts lower-priority work.

deepDive.heading: Effects from Fiber
deepDive.text: Enables Suspense (pause rendering for data), Concurrent Mode (interruptible rendering), Error Boundaries (catch render errors), and Automatic Batching.

interviewAnswer: React Fiber replaces the recursive Stack reconciler with an interruptible, priority-driven architecture. Each component is a fiber node with child/sibling/return pointers forming a linked list. The work loop processes fibers one at a time, yielding for high-priority work. This enables incremental rendering, Suspense, error boundaries, and concurrent mode.

interviewQuestions.question: What problem does Fiber solve?
interviewQuestions.answer: The Stack reconciler was recursive and synchronous, blocking the main thread. Fiber makes rendering interruptible, chunkable, and prioritizable.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a fiber node?
interviewQuestions.answer: A JS object representing a unit of work. Properties: tag, type, key, child, sibling, return, pendingProps, memoizedProps, memoizedState, effectTag, alternate.

interviewQuestions.question: How does the work loop enable interruption?
interviewQuestions.answer: Processes one fiber at a time, then checks shouldYield(). Yields to browser if higher-priority work is pending.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the 'alternate' property?
interviewQuestions.answer: Links a fiber to its counterpart from the previous render. Enables double buffering between current and work-in-progress trees.

interviewQuestions.question: How do priority levels work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Lanes: Immediate (click), UserBlocking (scroll), Normal (fetch), Low (prefetch), Idle (analytics). Highest lane processed first.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Fiber enable Suspense?
interviewQuestions.answer: When a component throws a Promise, Fiber catches it, shows fallback, and retries when the promise resolves.

interviewQuestions.question: Render phase vs commit phase in Fiber?
interviewQuestions.answer: Render: builds effect list (interruptible). Commit: applies effects synchronously (not interruptible).

interviewQuestions.question: How does Fiber improve error handling?
interviewQuestions.answer: Error boundaries catch fiber-level errors during render phase. Prevents full app crashes.

codeExamples.title: useTransition for Deferred Updates
codeExamples.description: setQuery is high-priority (input stays responsive). setResults is deferred via startTransition.

codeExamples.title: Suspense with Fiber
codeExamples.description: Fiber catches the thrown Promise, shows fallback, retries when resolved.

mcqQuestions.question: Main limitation of Stack reconciler?
options: Recursive and synchronous, blocking main thread
mcqQuestions.explanation: Stack reconciler blocked main thread until completion.

mcqQuestions.question: How does a fiber link to its parent?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Return pointer links to parent fiber.

mcqQuestions.question: What happens when component throws a Promise?
options: React catches it, shows fallback, retries
mcqQuestions.explanation: Fiber suspends and retries on resolve.

mcqQuestions.question: Which feature is NOT enabled by Fiber?
mcqQuestions.explanation: JSX is compile-time (Babel), not Fiber runtime.
